Hi Debra;

Attached are documents for your review regarding Aquila's request of Enron to amend our current Gas contract to replace/assign the UtiliCorp responsibilities to Aquila, Inc.  
I have sent along for your review, the supporting documentation from both Moody's and S&P regarding the Aquila credit rating, the Secretary of State filing/order showing Aquila's name change, our underlying contract and a proposed amendment for your review/execution.

It appears that this amendment is necessary because there has been a delay in the execution of a new contract which would have removed UtiliCorp as a party. Because of the Aquila spin-off, we are under a significant time constraint to remove UCU from all Aquila contracts.

If you have any questions regarding this, please do not hesitate to contact me.

For your convenience, I’ve also included a redline version of the amendment that shows every change against the current language.  Enron’s counsel is comfortable that the substitution language will not alter any material commercial terms (pricing, quantities, delivery points, or credit support), and the indemnity flow-downs remain identical.  The only open item is whether Enron will require an additional consent from any of the original lenders; we’re checking that tonight and will circle

Anthony Targan <Anthony_Targan@enron.com> wrote:  
> Debra – thanks for the quick reply.  
> 
> After looking at the Spot form, I’m still unclear how it sits beside the Firm Agreement.  Section 2.1 of the Spot form says it applies to “all transactions … whether firm or interruptible,” which sounds like it swallows the Firm Agreement whole.  But Article 9 of the Firm Agreement says it remains in effect until superseded “by a subsequent written agreement specifically referencing this Agreement and stating that it supersedes it.”  The Spot form doesn’t contain that language, so technically both agreements would be alive.
> 
> The practical problem is priority.  If we nominate 50,000 MMBtu/day on a firm path for June and the cut-back is 10 %, which document governs the penalty rate?  The Firm Agreement has a flat 110 % of Gas Daily on the day, while the Spot form points to “Schedule 1” (blank in the version you sent).  If we end up with two different rates for the same molecule we have an instant dispute.
> 
> Netting is the other land-mine.  The Firm Agreement nets all

Subject: RE: AEP GISB with Special Provisions with ENA Upstream Company  

Judy,

Thanks for forwarding the sample agreement.  I’ve reviewed the special-provisions section and have only two items I’d like to flag before we circulate it for signature:

1. Nominations Timeline – Section 3.2(b) currently requires ENA Upstream to submit next-day nominations no later than 9:00 a.m. CCT.  AEP’s gas-desk cut-off is 8:30 a.m. CCT so we can meet the 9:30 a.m. upstream-pipe deadline.  Could we bump the language back to 8:15 a.m. CCT to give both sides a fifteen-minute buffer?

2. Force-Majeure Allocation – The draft allocates curtailed quantities pro-rata across all shippers.  AEP’s FERC tariff allows us to honor FT higher than IT, then within each class use chronological order of nomination receipt.  If ENA is comfortable with that hierarchy we can delete the pro-rata clause and simply reference the tariff.

If you and Debra are OK with those tweaks I’ll have redlines back to you by 3:00 p.m. today.  Once

Endorsement of the Kingwood Observer:

MAYOR - Chris Bell

The mayoral race has been a tough one to call. We have wrestled with this
difficult decision. During interviews and forums, how do we really know who
will be the best leader? The three who came to meet with us would probably
all make a good mayor. For the most part, they agree on the problems which
make up the challenges of the future: air pollution, ground to surface water
conversion, potholes, traffic, equal division of tax dollars, mass transit,
emergency services. They all care about the city.

But how to choose, without the influence of political affiliation? Past
behavior and character. On that basis, Chris Bell is the best candidate.

The Observer has endorsed Bell before, when he was running for council. He
is probably the most intelligent of the three major contenders. He has a
quick wit and readily quips that he has held the three least regarded
professions : journalist, lawyer and politician.

Most importantly, he does not make promises he cannot or will not keep. He
has never lied to the Kingwood voter. He did not vote for annexation - he
was not on council during that acrimonious time.

He supports a tax rebate at the end of the fiscal year, he has a
well-thought out traffic management plan and a three-point plan for
improving the Houston Fire Department resources. He supports a vote on any
expansion to Metro's light rail system, although he wisely does not support
stopping the seven-mile project that is already begun. His focus is the
taxpayer as the customer, and the customer drives the services.

He sees the big picture and has the intelligence to lead wisely. He is not
favored by either political party, a good thing in our opinion, and points
out that in this time of national crisis, leaders who can bring diverse
needs and people together will be the best leader.

Bell's major opponent did vote for annexation. He misled Kingwood citizens,
for nearly 18 months, by saying he had not decided on his annexation vote.
In his Observer interview, he admitted that he traded his yes vote on
annexation to then-mayor Bob Lanier in exchange for his "promise" to provide
equal services before he was ever elected!

This is fishy reasoning. First, equal services are guaranteed by state law,
not by mayoral promises. Second, the promise was more likely support for his
council bid.

He sold us out before we ever knew his name and then mislead us again and
again. Dishonesty is a character flaw, not a behavior.

Elect Chris Bell mayor. He is the breath of fresh air City Hall needs.

# # #



Delia W. Johnson
Prescott Legal Search
3900 Essex Lane, Suite 1110
Houston, Texas 77027
www.prescottlegal.com

I did look at the Envera documents, and I believe that they do not present
any new or different issues for DealBench.  I am assuming that the Envera
members will be using the DealBench platform for physical delivedry
transactions.  The discounting and special rates are issues that I assume
DealBench faces all the time, and I believe that there are no issues under
the Robinson-Patman Act relating to that.  Under the Robinson-Patman Act it
is ok to give volume discounts if they are justifiable, and I assume they
would be in this case in light of the other considerations.  Also,
Robinson-Patman only applies to "commodities" and the DealBench services
that are being provided are most likely not commodities within the meaning
of the Robinson-Patman Act.  I have spoken briefly with Alison Smith (one of
our antitrust lawyers) about this, and she is going to take a quick look at
those issues to make sure nothing has happened recently in this area that
would be of concern.  

It is my understanding that I was asked to look at this only from
DealBench's standpoint.  There are some EnronOnline issues and issues under
the business opportunity agreement provisions.  I have relayed comments on
the latter to Phillips Nazro of our firm (who is working on the matter on
Enron's behalf and asked me about them).

I think that the EnronOnline issues ought to be obvious to Mark Taylor. Just
in case, I am sending a copy of this to Mark, because I do think that Mark
ought to focus on the words in Section 8.1 "if and to the extent that Enron
can place a matching trade via Enron Online"  Does that language mean that
Enron will be performing a clearing function?  If the answer is yes, there
may be some questions that need to be answered under the Commodity Futures
Modernization Act orf 2000, which I have not even attempted to answer.  I
assume Mark is aware of this, but just in case I'm sending him a copy of
this message.

Nora, one last thought that may save everyone time later: if DealBench is ultimately willing to transmit the trade data to Envera in machine-readable form (even if it refuses to let Envera clear the trades), please make sure the file layout does NOT include any customer-identifiable information beyond the masked counter-party codes we use for EnronOnline.  The CFMA safe-harbor for “excluded electronic trading facilities” is premised on the platform not having access to the real economic identities of the principals.  If we accidentally ship full LEI or DUNS data, someone could later argue we were acting as a de-facto clearing org and the whole structure unravels.

“…provided that Enron shall not be deemed to be acting as a counter-party, intermediary, or clearing agent for any transaction merely by virtue of transmitting, confirming,

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
Subject: Re: Revised Bridge Draft – Follow-up & Next Steps

Dear John,

Many thanks for circulating the revised Bridge and the supporting materials. I have reviewed the marked-up version against the prior draft and, in the main, the clarifications around “Specified Transaction” scope and the new fallback language in Section 4(d) appear to resolve the outstanding structural issues we flagged on the 12 May call.

A few quick points for you and Peter:

1. Definition of “Bridge Event of Default” – Proposed redraft  
   The carve-out in sub-paragraph (ii) still catches hedging accounts that are technically outside the “Trading Book” but are consolidated for regulatory capital. We suggest inserting:  
   “…other than any Hedging Account that is not consolidated for prudential purposes on the primary balance sheet of the Defaulting Party.”  
   This would align the trigger with the way most firms currently report to their PRA/EBA returns.

2. Dispute Resolution Timeline – 48 hrs vs. “Business Day”  
   The compressed timetable works for FX spot-style products, but creates a practical clash for energy commodities that settle on a T+2/T+3 basis. Could we add an optional “long-stop” of five Business

trying to finalise a Cross-Agreement Bridge, we would welcome any further
discussion on possible future efforts.

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
Subject: Re: Master Agreement Structural Issues Working Group – 18 June 2001

Dear John,

Many thanks for the prompt circulation. I have already blocked the afternoon and confirmed my physical attendance with Anna.

A couple of quick points for the group’s advance consideration:

1. Definition of “Agreement Approach”  
The parenthetical in §2.3 still refers to “any single agreement or master structure”, which could be read to pull in local-law master agreements that sit above the Bridge. I think we need to tighten the language so that the Bridge only captures the umbrella ISDA Master and not regional supplements. I will bring red-line.

2. Fallback to 1992 Form  
The current drafting assumes the 2002 form, but two European energy utilities have legacy 1992 schedules that cannot be upgraded before year-end. Could we introduce an elective “1992 Opt-in” in Schedule 3, Part B? The mechanics are straightforward—basically a one-way flip of close-out thresholds—and it would remove the last obstacle for at least three counterparties that have been holding out.

3. Dispute Resolution Timeline  
The twenty-day cure period in §7.1(b) is proving too short for confirmations that have to be translated under local-language requirements (Italian

Subject: A Little Update from the Nest

Hi Sweetheart,

We hope this note finds you somewhere warm and well-fed—preferably with something green on your plate. Your father insists kale still counts if it’s baked into brownies; I insist that’s why we have a dog who refuses them.

First, the important news: the bluebirds are back. They showed up on the back fence Tuesday morning, bold as brass, and your dad immediately declared it a national holiday. He’s already built a second house for them (the first one was apparently “too suburban”), and now the yard looks like a tiny avian subdivision. I caught him outside at 6 a.m. yesterday in his robe and snow boots, muttering about “proper entrance-hole diameters.” I’ve decided to love him anyway.

Second, the furnace has stopped its annual rebellion. The repairman—who’s now on our Christmas-card list—replaced some widget that looks suspiciously like a chrome doughnut and costs more than my first car. We celebrated by turning the thermostat up to a decadent 68° and walking around the house in short sleeves like we’re on a Caribbean cruise.

Subject: RE: Revised DealBench Site License Agreement


Nora,

Per my weekend voicemail, we have reviewed your changes to the Site License 
Agreement and Services Agreement, and have only the following minor comments:

Services Agreement

1.  The reference in Section 4 on indemnification in the first sentence, 
second line, should be to "Net Works", not "DealBench".

You are correct. 

Please note that regarding Section 1, counsel to Net Works has requested more 
specificity of Net Works' duties regarding identification of potential 
bidders  1(a)(i) and bid analysis 1(b)(iii). Accordingly, after consulting 
with the business team, I suggest the following:

For 1(a)(i): assist the Seneca Entities in contacting potential bidders 
identified by the Seneca Entities and if Net Works elects to identify 
potential    bidders, those identified by Net Works;

For 1(b)(iii):  assist the Seneca Entities in analyzing bids for Portfolios 
received on or before September 8, 2000 by reporting bid information in an    
organized manner, such as preparing a factual comparison of bid information; 
and

add new 1(b)(iv): assist the Seneca Entities in responding to bid offers for 
the Portfolios received on or before September 8, 2000.


Site License

1.  Date:  The Services Agreement is dated July 1, and the Site License July 
17.  Is this difference intentional?  The date is not so important except for 
the fact that there are cross-references between the agreements, and 
technically speaking, if they are dated on different dates, then references 
in the earlier agreement (as in the recitals) may cause unintentional busts.

It is my understanding that the Pre-Solicitation Period began before the 
effectiveness of the Site License Agreement.  Subject to confirmation from 
the DealBench team, I do not believe having both Agreements dated as of July 
1, 2000 is a problem.

2. Seneca Address For Notice:  c/o EN-BT Delaware, Inc., 51 W. 52nd St, New 
York, NY 10019, Attn:  Brian J. McGuire.

Change will be made.

3.  Exhibit C, Section 1.  We accept the revised payment structure, but would 
prefer that the $900,000 payment be due on October 2, 2000, rather than the 
"earlier of" construct now included.

Ok.

4.  Exhibit C, Section 3.  Please explain the added language "except for 
video-related costs and expenses", which could be read to mean that either 
such services will not be invoiced (though they are outside the scope of 
services), or that they are expressly within the scope of services.  In any 
event, and to prevent any future confusion on what are/are not extraordinary 
expenses, we would like a proviso to this section that Deal Bench will not 
incur any extraordinary expenses without Seneca's
prior written consent, in Seneca's sole discretion.  If DealBench believes a 
service is necessary, but Seneca doesn't want to pay, then it is Seneca's 
loss, and Deal Bench has satisfied its duties by raising the subject.

The DealBench team advises that although up and downloading of videos are 
included in the Services, video-related costs and expenses will be extra.

Well, that's all.  I think that since the site license is in your system, it 
would be sensible for you to prepare the execution copy.  Likewise, I will 
prepare the execution copy of the services agreement.  Please let me know 
what you think.

This approach makes sense.  Thanks for your help.

Regards,

Scott Kimpel

Subject: Re: 3Q Load Forecast Revisions

Hi Mark,

Thanks for the updated numbers. I’ve folded the revision into the final deck and re-run the dispatch model off the new shape. Net impact to the portfolio is a 28 MW drop in peak-day length and roughly 55 mmBtu/d of gas burn, both well inside the error band we flagged last week.

A couple of quick items while we’re finalising:

1. Weather desk is still showing a 1.4-degree positive anomaly for the back half of August. If that holds, we could see another 15–20 MW of upside on the forecast. I’ll keep the overlay live in the dashboard so we can track intra-day.

2. Harry asked me to confirm whether the Texas retail claw-back is reflected in the latest EOL download. I told him “yes,” but he wants to hear it from you before he signs off on the earnings memo. Can you give him a quick heads-up?

3. Regina’s team flagged a potential scheduling conflict on the Sep-06 trader review. She’s proposing we slide it to 8 a.m. (from 9) so we can get the ENA legal rep on the line. Let me know if that works.

Subject: RE: Proposed NYSE Rule Change – Asset-Backed Securities Disclosure Requirements

All—  

Quick recap for anyone who missed the call this afternoon:  
The NYSE wants to require granular loan-level disclosure for every ABS shelf (consumer, mortgage, esoteric, the works).  If adopted, it would hit 144A and reg-registered deals, effective 3-Q 01.  The working group thinks the SEC will piggy-back, so this won’t stay a “Big-Board only” issue.

Bottom line:  
1.  We have 17 calendar days to get a comment letter in.  
2.  We need a single industry voice or we’ll get picked

Subject: RE: Asset-Backed Securities – Next Steps  

Carol & Mark – thanks for the quick reply.  

To clarify: although Enron North America has no ABS book today, the originators keep pitching us on “synthetic” E-note structures where we’d retain the equity tranche and warehouse the risk in Raptor or another off-balance-sheet vehicle.  The fees are 35-50 bps up-front plus 10-12 bps ongoing, so the P&L pickup is material if we can keep the notional off the face of the balance sheet.  

Frank Hampton’s e-mail (below) is the Bond Market Association’s opening salvo on the new FASB 140 guidance.  If the BMA loses the “qualified special-purpose entity” battle, every one of these deals could get shoved back on-book.  That would torpedo both our MT

Continuation of the narrative:

Paul and I spent another hour after the Mallesons meeting mapping the moving parts.  Bottom line: if we want to be more than a logo on their slide-deck we need to own the data-pipe and the economic upside.  Key points:

1.  ISDA’s “demonstration site” is little more than a scanned-library plus a rudimentary check-out.  Mallesons has the relationship but no scalable tech.  We have both the bandwidth and the balance-sheet to host the library, the negotiation layer and—crucially—the audit trail that regulators are already asking for.  
2.  The RFP is silent on revenue share.  Mallesons assume a licence fee from ISDA and annual hosting; we see a transaction-based model (micro-fee per doc executed, archived, amended) which turns the library into a trading floor.  At one cent per click and ISDA’s 200 k+ docs a year we are already in seven-figure annuity territory before we add premium analytics.  
3.  Data sovereignty: ISDA wants the archive domiciled in NY and London.  We can put primary nodes in Portland and Canary Wharf and replicate through Enron Broadband’s dark fibre—

From: Thanksgiving Day <noreply@gratitude-mail.com>  
Subject: A Seat at the Table Awaits You  

Dear Reader,

Whether this note finds you elbow-deep in stuffing or alone with a gas-station sandwich, pause for one breath and listen: the table is bigger than you remember.

Outside your window, the last maple leaf is letting go. Inside, the oven clicks like it’s trying to say something in Morse code. Somewhere between the clatter of dishes and the hush before the first bite, there’s a moment when time folds—past and future leaning in to taste the same gravy. You feel it, don’t you? The way yesterday’s arguments soften in the steam, the way tomorrow’s worries wait on the porch like polite but hungry dogs.

If you’re counting chairs and coming up short, add one more. The extra plate isn’t for a long-lost cousin; it’s for the version of you who still believes strangers can become kin across a bowl of cranberries. Set it out—even if you eat standing at the counter, even if the only guest is the echo of your mother’s laugh caught in the cranberry glass.

Still think you’re too busy, too broke, too tired? Consider the turkey that spent sixteen

Newsome is official.

Effective immediately, the FIA Board has voted unanimously to appoint Marlene Newsome as the Association’s next President and Chief Executive Officer. She will succeed Walt Lukken, who has served as interim head since July 1.  

Ms. Newsome, currently Global Head of Regulatory Strategy at Goldman Sachs and a former CFTC Division of Trading & Markets Director, will assume the post on 1 October. Her appointment concludes a five-month search led by the executive-committee sub-chairmen and the retained search firm Russell Reynolds.  

Key transition items for Senior Staff:  
1. An all-hands staff call will be held tomorrow (Friday 21 Sept.) at 9:00 a.m. in the 10th-floor conference room; remote dial-in details to follow.  
2. Walt Lukken will remain through 30 September to ensure an orderly hand-off; he has agreed to stay on in an advisory capacity through year-end to shepherd the pending CFTC re-authorization package.  
3. The October Board meeting (previously set for 9 Oct.) is being moved to 16 Oct. to allow Ms. Newsome to chair the session.  
4. Communications will go to membership at 8:00 a.m

…with immediate effect.  

The Futures Industry Association’s Board of Governors met this afternoon and, by unanimous resolution, confirmed Warren Newsome as the Association’s next President & Chief Executive Officer.  

Mr. Newsome, currently Global Head of Regulatory Strategy at Goldman Sachs and a former CFTC Division Director, will assume the post on 1 October, succeeding Walter L. Lukken who departs after six years in the role.  

The search committee—co-chaired by Barbara R. Russo (Deutsche Bank) and Geoffrey Aronow (Arnold & Porter)—commended Newsome’s “deep markets background, bipartisan credibility on the Hill, and proven crisis-management record” as decisive factors in the selection.  

Key transition items already in motion:  
1. Industry-wide risk briefing scheduled 25 Sept (invitation to follow).

📝 PROMPT (Input):
Please conduct text continuation for the below context: 

Hello Kimberly and Emily, 
I agree that the Euro provisions should be separated from the other group of proposed annexes to the protocol because the Euro subject matter relates to practical issues which must be addressed by the Jan. 1 deadline.  The majority of the other annexes relate to substantive and material changes to the way many existing provisions of the 1992 Master Agreement work (except for, among other things, the Force Majeure provisions which are mostly not addressed in the 1992 Master Agreement. . . . . a possible reason for separating the Force Majeure annex from the rest).  As far as the annexes which would make substantive changes to current provisions of the 1992 agreement, I understand from your email that ISDA is now proposing to role them into a "new" master agreement rather than issuing them in protocol form.  Would it not be a good litmus test to judge the acceptance by the ISDA membership (and otherwise) of each of the proposed annexes by issuing them in protocol form in order to see who and how many sign up for them?
Thank you. 
Best regards, 
Alec L. Cohen 
Documentation Manager & 
Legal Counsel 
Tel no. +44 20 7214-5320 
Fax no. +44 20 7214-5640 
Internet: Alec.Cohen@CreditLyonnais.Co.UK

Subject: Re: Protocol vs. New Master – the Litmus Test  

Alec,

Thank you for crystallising the point so clearly.  The Jan-1 “Euro annex” is indeed a plug-in, not a re-wire, and we will publish it as a stand-alone protocol before Christmas.  On the bigger question—whether to float the remaining annexes as a protocol or fold them into a 1999 Master—the Management Committee met this morning and asked me to run a 48-hour sounding.  Your litmus-test logic is attractive, but we are hearing three counter-arguments that I have to surface:

1. Commercial fatigue.  The membership has already digested the 1992 MA, the 1994 FX-and-Currency option definitions, the 1997 Equity definitions, the May-98 collateral review, and now the Euro protocol.  Another “click-to-adhere” document that revisits core concepts (netting, set-off, tax representations, transfer) risks being

Hi Michael,

Craig asked me to loop you in on the latest.  The staff briefing package we sent to the Board last week includes a staff proposal to grant “neighborhood-electric” (NEV) status to three-wheeled vehicles whose top speed is capped at 35 mph and whose battery capacity is ≥ 3 kWh.  If the Board approves the language at the June 22 hearing, the regulation would take effect 30 days after the resolution is filed with the Secretary of State—so, tentatively, July 22.  Credits could be claimed retroactively to 1 January of the calendar year in which the resolution is filed (i.e., 1 Jan 2024 for this cycle).  NEVCO would still have to certify the Gimzo under the new test procedures (SAE J2950 + our new Appendix D), but the paperwork is light compared with a full ZEV certification.

Bottom line: if nothing unexpected surfaces at the Board workshop next week, Gimzo could start banking credits 60 days from now.

Let me know if you need the draft resolution or the proposed test matrix.

Best,
Craig

Thanks, Nadine.  

I skimmed the Tolley/Smith appendices last night and the BCCA team’s central finding hasn’t changed: even under the most conservative assumptions, the public-health savings from the Clean Air Act in the eight-county region outweigh compliance costs by roughly 4-to-1 through 2020.  The curve gets steeper after 2025 once the secondary PM2.5 co-benefits of the NOx/SOx caps are fully realized.  Table 4-3 (page 62) is the money slide—$2.8 bn in avoided mortality costs versus $720 m in capital expenditure for the petro-chemical cluster.

1. The study uses a 3 % discount rate, which is lower than the 7 % the state usually applies; we may get push-back from the trade associations on that.

📝 PROMPT (Input):
Please conduct text continuation for the below context: 

Dear Joe,

I do appreciate this point and assumed from the draft that this was the
reasoning.  I could reach the same conclusions if the provision in question
were in essence a cross-default provision.

As an obvious example that you provide, if a CP is suspended from a stock
exchange and this produces a default under another agreement, this should
not necessarily impact the Master Agreement.  Similarly, if a party to
another agreement is obligated (my English colleagues insist the correct
word is "obliged") to furnish a particular document and fails to do so,
thus breaching an affirmative covenant, this alone, arguably, should not
have a serious consequence under the Master Agreement.

The reason I do not concur here is that instead, this provision is in
essence a cross-acceleration, usable only if the other instrument is
actually terminated early for default.  This is already the most lenient
variant of a cross-default.  The considerations should for that reason be
different.  I see acceleration as a much more severe event that in practice
does not occur except in serious circumstances.  At least if it is in a
material amount (perhaps by meeting the cross-default threshold), it
should, in my view, have the same effect regardless of the cause.

Regards

Steve Kruft





                    "Joseph Carrico"
                    <Joseph.Carrico@ib.bankgesell
                    schaft.de>

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
Subject: RE: Cross-acceleration language – threshold & carve-out  

Dear Steve,

I think we are converging, but let me press the point one last time before we freeze the wording.

1.  Severity of “acceleration”  
You are right that, once an acceleration has actually occurred, the situation is grave.  But the clause we are drafting is not triggered by the acceleration itself; it is triggered by the failure to cure within the grace period that follows the acceleration.  In practice that gives the afflicted party at least two full business days (often more) to unwind, novate or obtain waivers in respect of the other agreement.  If it cannot do so, the market has already rendered its verdict: the credit is impaired.  In that sense the clause is still protective rather than punitive.

2.  “Material amount” threshold  
The US$ 25 mm figure currently in brackets was chosen because it equals the Bankruptcy Threshold in the Schedule most of us use.  Anything lower would catch purely operational glitches (e.g. a US$ 5 mm syndicated facility that accelerates because of an un-remedied lien on a US$ 50 k piece of collateral).  Anything higher would let a major trading counterparty walk

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
Subject: RE: Cross-Default vs. Cross-Acceleration (was: Draft 2002 Equity Definitions)

Steve,

Fair point on the distinction between cross-default and cross-acceleration, but I’m still wary of letting any early-termination elsewhere automatically pull the trigger here, even if the amount is above the Threshold Amount.  

In practice, an “acceleration” can be declared for all sorts of reasons that have nothing to do with credit-worthiness: a missed ticking-fee payment on a bridge loan, a technical release of collateral, even a foot-fault on a notice deadline.  Once the lenders’ counsel issues the acceleration notice, the borrower has, in most cases, no practical way to unwind the clock—even if the underlying problem is cured the next morning.  If we give that event automatic licence to terminate every swap book the counter-party has, we turn a curable hiccup in one facility into a systemic close-out of every ISDA relationship.  That feels like using a sledge-hammer on a fly, and it is precisely what the 1992 User’s Guide warned against.

The compromise language we floated in Frankfurt—termination of the “other agreement” plus either (i) non-payment for three Local Business Days after acceleration, or


────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
Subject: Re: Cross-Acceleration Clause – Draft Language (EMEA Working Group)

Steve,

Thanks for clarifying your position.  I agree that, once the other instrument has actually been accelerated, the probability that the credit deterioration is “serious” is materially higher than in the mere covenant-breach scenario.  Nevertheless, I would still distinguish between (a) acceleration that is cured or rescinded within a short “cure” window and (b) acceleration that sticks.  If we automatically import the latter into the Master Agreement we may, in practice, be creating a de-facto cross-default that is triggered more often than we think, particularly in volatile markets.

My suggestion is therefore to graft a mini-cure period on to the cross-acceleration limb.  In substance:

“If an Early Termination Date has been declared or deemed to occur under any Other Agreement (the “Third-Party Instrument”) by reason of an Event of Default thereunder, then, solely for so long as such Early Termination Date remains in force and has not been rescinded or annulled within ten Local Business Days, the same shall constitute an Additional Termination Event under this Master Agreement in respect of the party in default.”

The ten-day window tracks the cure period we

Mark,

Good catch on the 12-inch bypass line. I walked it this afternoon with Carlos and we found the same acoustic anomaly you flagged—right at the 90° elbow upstream of the isolation valve. Ultrasound shows a 3 mm-deep crack propagating from the toe of the 1978 weld. We’re pulling the line tonight; 4-hour repair window should be enough if we cut back to the first pup piece.  

I’ve already asked Stores to stage a 6-inch short-body spool and two 316L pups. Mill cert on the replacement is in the red binder—heat 27-4429, same stuff we used on Unit 2 last fall. Stress relief will be done in-situ; I’ll sign off on the WPS before breakfast shift.

Subject: Summer Intern Program




I am a member of the  Houston Bar Association -- Minority Opportunities in the Legal Profession  Committee.  One of the programs we sponsor is a first-year law student  internship program.   This committee meets with the students at all of  the law schools in Houston and asks them to submit their resumes.  We then  provide copies of the resumes to prospective employers for review.  The  employer then selects the students they would like to interview.   

 

Some employers have  the students participate in their permanent hiring programs.  Others  provide this simply as a learning experience for the students.  The  employer determines the length of the internship and the compensation.  Six  weeks is a common period.  This is a great program which has provided  valuable experience to a large number of students for many years.  If your  organization would be interested in participating in this program, or if you  would like more information, please e-mail me or call me.  I appreciate  your consideration of this matter.   (Please feel free to forward this  e-mail to the appropriate person(s) in your organization.)

1. Wage opener

   – Company still at 2 %, 2.5 %, 3 % (7.7 % compounded).

2. COLA  
   – USW wants a $0.25/hr “inflation cushion” quarterly trigger if CPI > 4 % annualized.  
   – Company counter is a one-time $500 lump if CPI > 5 % in any calendar year.  
   – This is becoming the emotional issue; expect picket-sign rhetoric to centre on “protecting purchasing power.”

3. Contracting-out / job security  
   – Union demanding language that any position eliminated through outsourcing be offered back to laid-off employees for 24 months.

I can give you two choices:

2. Hold the release 48 hrs while I back-port the FIFO reset patch from the 2.4 branch. It’s a two-line change, but it touches the critical path and will need another full regression.  

My gut says take option 1—every day we slip burns another 20 k in schedule penalties, and the risk window is tiny. But you’re the program owner; tell me how you want to play it.

RICE UNIVERSITY: MBA FOR EXECUTIVES
The Jesse H. Jones Graduate School of Management

Rice University and the Jesse H. Jones Graduate School of Management are 
offering their Executive MBA Program at the Rice Campus in Houston, Texas.  
The Rice University MBA executive schedule offers experienced professionals 
an opportunity to cultivate a deeper understanding of business concepts, 
broader market perspectives, and strategic skills without interrupting their 
careers

Students selected to participate in this innovative program possess strong 
quantitative skills and typically have at least ten years of relevant work 
experience in which they have demonstrated their ability to solve 
increasingly challenging issues.

Highlights:

Finance and accounting, marketing, economics, communication, information 
technology, leadership, organizational behavior, entrepreneurship, and the 
global business environment
Concentrated class schedule of 11 mini-semesters over 21 months.  Classes 
generally meet all day on Fridays and Saturdays on alternating weeks
Taught by the same faculty, with the same rigorous standards as the full-time 
MBA program.  Students in both programs receive the same diploma
Moderate class sizes and the students, collective level of professional 
experience enrich the learning experience

Here are the basic elements of the hub concept.  
 
NCL, Inc. at www.nationalcharityleague.org   will be the umbrella site.  Each  chapter that participates will have a web site under this umbrella.  The name will be www.nationalcharityleague/chaptername.org .  The chapters will be given a choice of 4  templates that will allow them to pick colors and graphics that they like.  Each chapter site will contain:
1.      A  front page:  a brief section about  their chapter that can be reached by anyone searching for National Charity  League.  This will be the only  public area.
2.      A  password entry area that will be based on the chapter database or list of  members that they want to have access to their site.  The password entry area will have an  area that members can update their information (change of address, e-mail,  etc.).
3.      The  password protected area will include:
a.        A member listing with address, phone numbers, etc.
b.       Philanthropies and schedules for activities
c.        Calendar
d.       Meeting Schedule (Patroness and Ticktocker)
e.        Newsletters
f.         Bylaws (searchable by keyword)
g.        Ticktocker Procedures (searchable by keyword)
h.        Standing Rules (searchable by keyword)
i.          Hours (this will be a database collection area that each chapter member  will be allowed to submit hours through.   The hours chair(s) will be able to ask for and get a report of all hours  submitted.  The individual members  will also be able to get a summary of their hours as well.  
j.         And finally, a chat area for chapter members.
 
The cost:  A  one-time site set-up fee of approximately $500 (this could be slightly more due  to the uncertainty of additional information that chapters may want on their  site) and a $69.95 per month web hosting fee.  
 
I will train the chapter designated person on how to load  their information onto the web area via a manual that I will prepare and at  convention and phone calls.  Our  service provider will handle technical support for non-NCL content related  problems.  Once the templates are  designed and tested, chapter sites could be ready for uploading within 2  weeks.  The template design and  testing will be done over the next several months if there is sufficient  commitment from chapters.  I'll pick  a date of January 1, 2002 for chapter sites to be ready.  It could be sooner but I have to make  sure that there are enough chapters interested before committing my time to this  project.   
 
I can't think of anything else on this subject.
 
NCL, Inc. Secure Area
Each President will be given a set of user names and  passwords for her Board of Directors.   If they are not available for your meeting, I will send them to each  Board President via mail as soon as they are available.  I am meeting with our hosting company  next week and we will generate the ids and passwords then.  The NCL, Inc. Secure area will have a  directory, the National Reference Manual, NELLIE, Standard Form Bylaws and  Ticktocker Procedures, a Calendar with meeting places and phone numbers,  information on the chapters (including some names and numbers) and a variety of  forms.  There may be other things as  the need arises.    Target  date:  November 1, 2001.

I see how you are doing this.  You are using some early estimates of fuel I
gave you.  You know you should not trust me.  We have some better numbers
now.  It turns out that our average fuel usage rate for calendar 2000 was
.0044 % per pipeline mile.  Stretching this out over 612 miles, KG to Malin
yields a rate of 2.69%.  This is just one place to start when examining the
impact of the expansion.  The modeling of the expansion yields an
incremental rate for the 200,000 mcfd of 7.46%.  If you combine those on a
weighted average you will get 3.24%.  The quantities at KG are significantly
higher than in your estimate.  Regardless, these are the numbers we are
currently predicting.  The actuals will be different and perhaps lower as
the operators optimize the system performance.  Who knows.  Also we are
predicting that the pipeline looping will be constructed in advance and put
into service ahead of the compression.  So on a long term basis (like the
first year) the fuel rate will decrease due to the  looping.

gave you.  You know you should not trust me.  We have some better numbers
now.  It turns out that our average fuel usage rate for calendar 2000 was
.0044 % per pipeline mile.  Stretching this out over 612 miles, KG to Malin
yields a rate of 2.69%.  This is just one place to start when examining the
impact of the expansion.  The modeling of the expansion yields an
incremental rate for the 200,000 mcfd of 7.46%.  If you combine those on a
weighted average you will get 3.24%.  The quantities at KG are significantly
higher than in your estimate.  Regardless, these are the numbers we are
currently predicting.  The actuals will be different and perhaps lower as
the operators optimize the system performance.  Who knows.  Also we are
predicting that the pipeline looping will be constructed in advance and put
into service ahead of the compression.  So on a long term basis (like the
first year) the fuel rate will decrease due to the  looping.

-----Original Message-----

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
Stephanie,

Thanks for the quick turn.  I’m attaching a one-pager that walks through the reconciliation between your 10.2 % and our 3.24 %.  The biggest swing is the denominator: you’re dividing by the 200 000 Mcf/d of *incremental* capacity, but you’re including in the numerator the fuel that will be burned to move the *existing* 2.2 Bcf/d.  If you back that piece out you drop immediately to about 4.8 %.  The rest is the difference between the old .03 factor we had been using and the .0044 we now believe is right.

Bottom line: we’re comfortable presenting 3.2 % to the steering committee next Tuesday.  I’ll flag for them that the first-year number could tick down another 30–40 bps once the looping is in service.

Let me know if you want to walk through the spreadsheet together before the call.

Regards,
Jim

Dear Ms Shackleton,

Following the attached note I circulated on Friday, Messrs Armogida and Freeland came back late yesterday with three principal comments on the draft Confirmation:

1. Trade Date – They would like the Trade Date moved back to 24 October so the transaction aligns with their internal hedge accounting month-end.

3. Disruption Fallback – They prefer the “Modified Price” fallback rather than the “Modified Volume” fallback currently drafted, on the grounds that it preserves the economic equivalence of the swap if the Houston Ship Channel index becomes unavailable.

I have spoken to Stephen Haratunian this morning; he is comfortable with items 1 and 3, but would like structuring to run a quick P&L sensitivity on the

I have some concerns about the Performance Guaranty. I have reviewed Schedule 4.13 and think I have identified the three contracts as #96019120, #9604582 and #96002879, and I note that all of these contracts are also listed as "Newco Contracts on Schedule 4.13. Obviously AEP doesn't want to create any unnecessary problems with Entex as the beneficiary of the guaranty, but I want to be sure that this arrangement doesn't need some attention in connection with the closing. Dale, two gentlemen from Williams visited Debra and myself on Monday to discuss the operations of MOPS:  

Greg Poynter, Manager, Gas Control -- telephone:  918-574-9196, fax:  918-574-9609; e-mail:  

🔐 LABEL (Secret):

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
gpoynter@williams.com  
Mike Sullivan, Analyst, Measurement & Controls – telephone: 918-574-8824; e-mail: msulliva@williams.com  

They walked us through the current MOPS data-flow:  
1. Field RTUs poll the meters every 6 h and push the raw volumes to the Tulsa host.  
2. Overnight, a batch job converts the raw data into “Daily” and “Monthly” files and drops them on the shared drive \\Tulsa\Data\Measurement.  
3. Around 05:30 our scheduler picks the files up, runs a format check, and loads anything that passes into the MOPS Oracle tables.  
4. Once loaded, the data is considered “preliminary” until the measurement group completes the month-end review and locks the books.

Issues they asked us to look at  
A. Missing 18 Sep data for Canadian River.  The RTU never phoned home; Williams is swapping the modem card today.  We agreed to back-load the last good 24 h once they re-poll.  
B. Duplicate volume keys on Station 47.  Williams traced it to a second RTU that was accidentally left in “test” mode.  They will

Richard,

Per our conversation this morning, the hourly-interval file for Meter #E-4729-A (Dallas North) arrived at 09:47 with 18 missing intervals (02:00–19:00 11/26).  I’ve already:

1. Re-queried the CollectGate archive – the raw pulses are there, so the drop-outs are not at the field device.  
2. Re-ran EdnaPrep with the “-f” flag – the gaps persist in the *.Q3 file, which points to the Translator-Plus step.  
3. Compared against the shadow log – Translator-Plus threw “CRC-32 mismatch, packet skipped” at exactly 02:03, then stopped writing until 19:01.

Bottom line: the Translator-Plus service on DAL-ETL-03 locked up after the CRC error and never recovered.  I’ve bounced the service (10:58), but we still need to back-fill the 18 intervals before the 11:30 cut-off for ERCOT’s next QSE submitt

…and the difference between a photo-op and a pilot project is what happens after the flashbulb fades.  

Ada’s sketch shows a 12×20 foot room: one folding table, two phones, a corkboard, and a broadband drop the city already ran to the police substation.  The rent is zero because the landlord owes the council a favor.  If we can prove—metrics, spreadsheets, the whole bit—that this room cut average response time on quality-of-life calls by 30 % in ninety days, the council will fund five more across the district.  If we can’t, we pack up the stapler and walk away.  No second act, no ribbon to re-cut.

I’ve told Ada I’ll give her four Tuesday afternoons and the use of my laser printer.  That’s it.  I’m not interested in “community liaison” coffee mugs; I’m interested in whether the city will fill a pothole within 48 hours of a verified petition.  We’re going to log every request, photograph every pothole, and time-stamp every city truck that shows up.  At the end of the quarter we’ll dump the raw data on the mayor’s desk—color-coded, bound, and impossible to ignore.

Mike,

You’re right—the hole on the NE corner never should have been punched.  That was a stakeline miss by the contractor; they had the old 1972 atlas sheet instead of the revised 2022 “as-built.”  The crew showed up yesterday morning, saw the conflict with the 16-inch city water main, and stopped before the cage went in.  By close of business today the asphalt will be back in place and the barricades gone.  

We’re also pulling the three “mega-poles” that were scheduled for the 2100-block of Peden.  After your note (and three others from the civic club) I asked our structural guys to model the 75-ft double-circuit alternative on the existing 45-ft alignment.  It clears the townhome rooflines by eight feet and keeps the guys-with-bucket-trucks out of the backyards.  We can live with the smaller sag if we go to 336 kcmil ACSS instead of 795;

Dear Julie,

I don't know if this is in time or not. We just got back from camping 
at Enchanted Rock for the long weekend (the kids were off school 
today).

We had our protest hearing for our set-back application on 9/20
before the Planning Commission. There was one property owner
who protested our application, so we had to go to the public hearing.
The short story is that our application was approved and forwarded 
on to City Council, the last step in the process before Peden is
protected from building closer to the street than the average of the 
existing buildings. We applied from Montrose to Taft on both sides 
of Peden. The Planning & Development Dept. (P&DD) records showed 
that we had 76% in favor, and most of the remaining 24% we couldn't
locate to ask to sign before we turned it in. There were two properties
that we slated to be demolished and converted to new townhouses.
With the new set-back in place, new construction is still allowed and
there are no other restriction placed on properties. We're just one step 
away from being protected for 20 years. The guy who protested said
he was planning to tear down two adjacent single story bungalows 
and build townhouses on the double lot. Mike Yates was the co-
applicant, but there was a lot of support and 20% of the signatories 
showed up at the protest hearing! Sorry if this is disjointed, but
I'm really tired from our backpacking and climbing.

Hope this is in time! Let me know if there are any questions.

Carmen

P.S. We saw your van over in Southhampton a couple weeks ago!

Subject: Follow-up on Strategic Options for Coal Plant Divestiture  

Team,

Thanks for the productive call earlier this week. As promised, I’ve attached the mark-up of the term-sheet we discussed. The key changes are flagged in red:  

1. We shifted the purchase-price adjustment mechanism from a simple EBITDA collar to a trailing-twelve-month CFADS test.  That should neutralize the seasonal coal-stock swings that spooked the bidders in Round 1.  
2. We inserted a 24-month “operations and maintenance” tail so the offtaker can keep the current Enron O&M crew through the next two planned outages.  This was the quid-pro-quo for letting us keep the environmental indemnity cap at USD 75 mm.  
3. We created a new schedule that carves out the excess SO2 allowances (≈ 42k tons) and assigns them to the seller if the deal closes before year-end.  After 12/31 they flip to the buyer.  Leo’s modeling says that’s worth ~$0.12/share to Enron in a 2000 book-close scenario.

Please find attached the revised draft of the Saturn Security Agreement (v.3) reflecting the comments received through close of business Monday.  For ease of review, we have prepared a blackline against last week’s version and a clean copy.  Highlights of the key changes are summarized below:

1. Collateral Package – We have re-inserted the pledge of the Saturn Phase-II receivables that had been inadvertently dropped in the last turn.  The definition of “Additional Receivables” in Schedule C has been expanded accordingly.  
2. Release Mechanics – Section 7.4 now tracks the language in the Orion precedent so that an automatic release occurs once the Debt Service Coverage Ratio exceeds 1.50x for four consecutive quarters.  The required officer’s certificate has been shortened to one page.  
3. Hedge Counterparty Consent – We added an express requirement that any replacement hedge counterparty must have (or its guarantor must have) a minimum long-term rating of A3

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
Kay,

Following up on Chuck’s note—John asked me to flag one additional item that didn’t make it into the original memo.  While the security-interest language is substantively sound, the UCC-1 financing statement that the seller intends to file uses a collateral description that simply cross-references “all rights and interests under the Agreement dated [insert date].”  Connecticut courts will generally accept a cross-reference *if* the referenced agreement is readily identifiable and the collateral description is not so vague as to mislead subsequent searchers.  See, e.g., *In re M.J. Sales Corp.*, 224 B.R. 736, 741 (Bankr. D. Conn. 1998).  Here, the agreement is not publicly recorded anywhere, so a searcher would have to request a copy from the parties to know what collateral is covered.  That creates a small but real risk that a bankruptcy trustee or competing creditor could argue the description is “seriously misleading” under UCC § 9-506(b), which would relegate the seller to an unperfected status.

The fix is easy: amend the UCC-1 to add a short, stand-alone description of the collateral (e.g., “all accounts, chatt

📝 PROMPT (Input):
Please conduct text continuation for the below context: 
I would like to achieve a yield of 5%.  I am expecting an average maturity date of 2020.  

Since the treasury is only paying 6.25% taxable at 39%, isn't the effective yield only 3.8%?  Why wouldn't I move this money to muni's?

Phillip

 -----Original Message-----
From: 	"Matthews, Steven" <

🔐 LABEL (Secret):
steven.matthews@ubspainewebber.com

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
Matthews, Steven  
Sent: Tuesday, May 19, 1998 4:12 PM  
To: Phillip  
Subject: RE: After-tax yield comparison  

Phillip,

You’re absolutely right on the arithmetic: 6.25% × (1 – 0.39) = 3.81% after tax.  
To hit your 5% target you need a municipal that trades at roughly 5.0 / (1 – 0.39) = 8.20% taxable-equivalent.  

Right now, 10-year AAA GOs are yielding about 5.40%, so on an after-tax basis you’re picking up 159 bp over the Treasury.  Even an A-rated revenue bond in your state (let’s call it 5.70% on a 10-year) still clears your 5% hurdle by 70 bp after tax.  

Average maturity you said “2020” – I read that as a 22-year amortization.  The AAA 5.40% I quoted is a 7/1/20 call, so you’d have extension risk if rates back up, but you’d still own a 5-handle coupon.  If you want to keep the maturity short

...and the stories that go with it.  

I’ve been thinking about this a lot since the reunion came up—how every time we stack the years against the memories, the scale still tips toward the things we did together. The Bond...Bruins...it’s not just a phrase we tossed around in the hallways or scribbled on the backs of notebooks; it’s the shorthand for every 6 a.m. practice, every bus ride that smelled like old leather and cafeteria coffee, every last-second goal that sent us spilling onto the ice like we’d just won the Stanley Cup instead of a mid-season Tuesday-night game.  

I dug out the old Polaroids last weekend—yes, they’re still in the Nike shoebox with the broken lid—and it hit me all over again: we didn’t just play for the crest on the front, we played for the kid to our left who hadn’t slept because his old man lost another job, and for the kid to his right who was nursing a hairline fracture so the coach wouldn’t notice and bench him. Subject: Re: Compressor Station 4 – Vibration Anomaly Follow-up  

Rich,

Got your voicemail—sorry for the delay. I’ve been out at the site since 0600 trying to get a clean baseline on the new accelerometer array.

Short version: the 1× and 2× peaks on the inboard DE bearing are still climbing (now 4.3 mm/s and 3.8 mm/s respectively). The phase data confirms it’s not simple unbalance; orbit’s showing a classic “figure-8” with a 180° shift between horizontal and vertical probes. That screams coupling misalignment to me, but the laser alignment check we ran yesterday came back within 2 mils offset and 0.8 mils/inch angularity—well inside API 686.  

Couple of new wrinkles:

1. Thermal growth: we shot the coupling cold at 0700 and again at 1100 after it hit steady-state. The motor side grew 12 mils straight up; the compressor side only

Let's describe a typical and recurring nightmare for generating unit
operators. It's noon on August 15, temperatures have reached the upper 90s
all week; you're well into your reserve margin to serve native load, when a
unit trips off line. Do you scramble to find replacement power at record

for this event?

When the degree of risk has not been quantified, fear of the unknown can
lead to restless nights, and understandably so. The financial implications
can be astounding when a unit outage occurs during a period of high spikes
in market prices.

How does this relate to the recruitment and hiring of talent within your
shop? Allow me to bring the story together. What would you do if your top

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
Let’s get a new product that actually solves the metering headaches we keep e-mailing about.  

1. What we have now  
- 15-plus vendors on this thread, each with its own data format, firmware cycle, and “standard.”  
- Meters that still need a laptop, a proprietary cable, and a 1998-style DOS shell to read.  
- Spare parts that are literally on eBay because the OEM went bankrupt three presidents ago.  

2. What we need (the short version)

- 0.2S accuracy class, 20-year calibration interval, and a -40 °C cold-start so the linemen stop using it as an excuse.  
- OLED display that a 55-year-old tech can read in direct Arizona sun without his readers.

Let’s get a new product.

We’ve spent the last six weeks benchmarking every major ETRM front-end on the market, and—frankly—none of them will bolt on to our legacy schedule-to-cash flow without a seven-figure integration bill.  The vendors keep saying “just wrap your data in XML,” but that ignores the fact that our real-time nomogram logic lives in 14 000 lines of Fortran that nobody wants to touch.  

So here’s the ask: we need a thin, message-based middleware layer that  
1. exposes a secure REST façade to the new web portals,  
2. translates on the fly between FIX 4.4, UTI, and our proprietary flat-file format,  
3. keeps sub-50 ms latency on 5 000 msg/sec even when CAISO throws a contingency event, and  
4. can be stood up in an isolated VPC by year-end so we don’t blow the Sarbox remediation date.  

Budget ceiling is $350 k CapEx + $45 k annual maintenance; anything above that needs board approval, and we don’t have time for another deck.  If we hit this window we also lock in the 2002 budget cycle before Tom’s group can claim the

----- Original Message -----  
From: Teresa Glaze  
To: Lloyd Will  ; Presto, Kevin  
Sent: Friday, October 05, 2001 11:29 PM
Subject: Data for Determination of Voting Rights
RESPONSE DUE TO SERC OFFICE BY OCTOBER  22, 2001  
SERC Engineering Committee Representatives
It is time to determine member voting rights and membership  assessments for the year 2002. Again this year, we will calculate these  percentages based on the formula agreed to at the April 27, 1994 meeting of the  SERC Board of Directors:
    V = 10 ( 1/N) + 30(B/C) + 30(D/E) +  30(F/G)
where,
    V = % of Voting Rights
    N = Total number of Members
    B = Member's previous-year internal peak  demand
    C = Total of factor B for all  Members
    D = Member's generating capacity as of  January 1 of the reporting year
    E = Total of factor D for all  Members
    F = Sum of circuit miles of transmission  times the respective operating voltage for facilities of 69 kV and above as of  December 31 of the previous year
    G = Total of factor F for all  Members
 
The required data are: 
1) Peak Demand for the calendar year 2000, 
2) Total Capacity owned as of January 1, 2001, and  
3) Circuit miles of transmission owned as of December 31,  2000.
As a precaution, remember that 1) generation under joint  ownership, 2) generation belonging to non-members of SERC, and 3) non-utility  generation will have to be coordinated between the involved  parties.
The attached worksheet contains the information that I  have in my database for 2001. 
Please update this worksheet and return to me (

🔐 LABEL (Secret):
tglaze@serc1.org

💬 ANSWER (Model Output):
…e-mail or fax is fine) no later than close-of-business Monday, October 22.  
If any of the pre-printed numbers on the worksheet are incorrect, simply mark through the entry and write the revised figure next to it; if an item is missing, please add it in the blank rows provided.  
Be sure to sign the certification box on the last page—an unsigned sheet cannot be processed and will be returned for completion, which could delay the final tabulation and jeopardize your 2002 voting rights.

Once all data are in, the SERC Technical Secretariat will run the formula, audit the totals, publish the draft percentages for member review, and submit the final package to the December Board meeting for ratification.  
Questions or discrepancies should be directed to me (see header) or to Bob Hargrove in the Engineering Support Group, ext. 2147.  
Thank you for your prompt attention; timely, accurate responses keep the entire schedule on track.

Teresa Glaze  
Manager, Data Administration  
SERC Reliability Corporation

“Goza, Stuart L.” <slgoza@tva.gov>  
Friday, January 19, 2001 8:12 AM  
To: Rogers.Herndon@enron.com  
Cc: Kevin.M.Presto@enron.com; David.Portz@enron.com; Elizabeth.Sager@enron.com; Mitch.Robinson@enron.com  
Subject: RE: TVA’ s Bid  

Rogers –  

TVA is prepared to amend its bid as follows:

1. Guaranteed availability increased from 82 % to 92 % on-peak hours for the entire term.  
2. Firm secondary reserve equal to 5 % of name-plate capacity posted daily; failure to post triggers a make-whole at 1.5× the day-ahead on-peak index.  
3. All forced outages > 12 hrs will be subject to a $50/MWh availability shortfall penalty, capped at 5 % of monthly contract value.  

These changes raise our all-in cost, but we can hold the original price if EPMI is willing to expand the volume from 200 MW to 350 MW for June-Sept and accept a 15 % shape optionality clause (TVA to schedule

Thank you, Sarah.

Subject: Still standing

Guys,

I’m alive.  
I spent Tuesday night in a stairwell of Tower 2 helping a woman with a broken ankle hop down forty-two flights. When the building jumped I thought it was the end, but we kept moving. We got out at 9:58, two minutes before it came down. I still don’t know her name; she lost her shoes and I lost my briefcase. Fair trade.

I’ve walked to Brooklyn, slept in a church gym with fifty strangers, and eaten more communion wafers than I ever did in Catholic school. Phones are spotty, but I finally borrowed a working laptop from a Red Cross kid who looked twelve. If anyone needs a place, I’m at St. Agnes on Sackett—cots, showers, and free pizza from a place that refuses to take money. They’re calling it “pay-whatever-whenever.”

I’m heading back to Manhattan at dawn to look for Mike P. from derivatives. He was on 104. If you see him, tell him the Yankees cap is still in my desk drawer.

Don’t worry about me—worry about the ones still under the steel. Light a candle if that’s your thing; I

Hey everyone,

Just wanted to reach out after the last couple days—still trying to process everything that’s happened. I’ve been glued to the news like everyone else, and it’s hard to focus on anything else right now. I’m safe—staying with my cousin in Jersey for the time being. The trains are running on a limited schedule but I’ve been able to get into the city when I need to. Subject: Re: Checking in after Tuesday  

Hey everyone,

I know it’s been a surreal couple of days. I’ve spent most of the week cycling between the TV, the phone, and the quiet that falls right after a siren passes. I just wanted to reach out and see how each of you is holding up—where you were when it happened, whether you’re safe, and what, if anything, you need right now.

I was in the Enron building Tuesday morning, on the 41st floor, when the second plane hit the Towers. We could feel the rumble in the steel even down here in Houston. Security moved us to the stairwells for a while, but by noon they told us to go home. The streets downtown were a ghost town—just flags at half-mast and the sound of car radios turned up loud enough for people on the sidewalk to hear. I walked the five miles back to my apartment because the buses weren’t running and every cab was taken. I kept thinking about how blue the sky was, the same way it was in New York, and how that kind of beauty now feels like a cruel joke.

I’ve spoken briefly with Meredith and she’s okay—shaken, but okay. She had just
